[Sino-Tibetan](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_languages "Sino-Tibetan languages")  * [Sinitic](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages")   + [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language")     - [Min](/wiki/Min_Chinese "Min Chinese")       * [Coastal Min](/wiki/Min_Chinese#Coastal_Min "Min Chinese")         + **Southern Min**

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Southern+Min%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Southern+Min%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Southern+Min%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Southern+Min%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): [闽南语](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%97%BD%E5%8D%97%E8%AF%AD "wikt:闽南语"); [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): [閩南語](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%96%A9%E5%8D%97%E8%AA%9E "wikt:閩南語"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Mǐnnányǔ*; [Pe̍h-ōe-jī](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "Pe̍h-ōe-jī"): *Bân-lâm-gí/gú*; lit. 'Southern Min language'), **Minnan** ([Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") pronunciation: [[mìn.nǎn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin "Help:IPA/Mandarin")) or **Banlam** (Min Nan Chinese pronunciation: [[bàn.lǎm]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")), is a group of linguistically similar and historically related [Chinese languages](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") that form a branch of [Min Chinese](/wiki/Min_Chinese "Min Chinese") spoken in [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") (especially the [Minnan region](/wiki/Minnan_region "Minnan region")), most of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") (many citizens are descendants of settlers from Fujian), Eastern [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"), and Southern [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang").[[5]](#cite_note-6) Southern Min dialects are also spoken by descendants of [emigrants from these areas](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") in [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora"), most notably in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), such as [Singapore](/wiki/Singaporean_Hokkien "Singaporean Hokkien"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Southern_Peninsular_Malaysian_Hokkien "Southern Peninsular Malaysian Hokkien"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippine_Hokkien "Philippine Hokkien"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Medan_Hokkien "Medan Hokkien"), [Brunei](/wiki/Ethnic_Chinese_in_Brunei "Ethnic Chinese in Brunei"), [Southern Thailand](/wiki/Thai_Chinese#Hokkien "Thai Chinese"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_Myanmar#Hokkien "Chinese people in Myanmar"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodian_Hokkien "Cambodian Hokkien"), [Southern and Central Vietnam](/wiki/Hoa_people#Ancestral_affiliations "Hoa people"), as well as major cities in the United States, including [in San Francisco](/wiki/Chinese_in_San_Francisco "Chinese in San Francisco"), [in Los Angeles](/wiki/Chinese_in_Los_Angeles "Chinese in Los Angeles") and [in New York City](/wiki/Chinese_in_New_York_City "Chinese in New York City"). Minnan is the most widely-spoken branch of Min, with approximately 34 million native speakers as of 2025.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

**Southern Min** ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): [闽南语](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%97%BD%E5%8D%97%E8%AF%AD "wikt:闽南语"); [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): [閩南語](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%96%A9%E5%8D%97%E8%AA%9E "wikt:閩南語"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Mǐnnányǔ*; [Pe̍h-ōe-jī](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "Pe̍h-ōe-jī"): *Bân-lâm-gí/gú*; lit. 'Southern Min language'), **Minnan** ([Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") pronunciation: [[mìn.nǎn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin "Help:IPA/Mandarin")) or **Banlam** (Min Nan Chinese pronunciation: [[bàn.lǎm]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")), is a group of linguistically similar and historically related [Chinese languages](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") that form a branch of [Min Chinese](/wiki/Min_Chinese "Min Chinese") spoken in [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") (especially the [Minnan region](/wiki/Minnan_region "Minnan region")), most of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") (many citizens are descendants of settlers from Fujian), Eastern [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"), and Southern [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang").[[5]](#cite_note-6) Southern Min dialects are also spoken by descendants of [emigrants from these areas](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") in [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora"), most notably in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), such as [Singapore](/wiki/Singaporean_Hokkien "Singaporean Hokkien"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Southern_Peninsular_Malaysian_Hokkien "Southern Peninsular Malaysian Hokkien"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippine_Hokkien "Philippine Hokkien"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Medan_Hokkien "Medan Hokkien"), [Brunei](/wiki/Ethnic_Chinese_in_Brunei "Ethnic Chinese in Brunei"), [Southern Thailand](/wiki/Thai_Chinese#Hokkien "Thai Chinese"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_Myanmar#Hokkien "Chinese people in Myanmar"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodian_Hokkien "Cambodian Hokkien"), [Southern and Central Vietnam](/wiki/Hoa_people#Ancestral_affiliations "Hoa people"), as well as major cities in the United States, including [in San Francisco](/wiki/Chinese_in_San_Francisco "Chinese in San Francisco"), [in Los Angeles](/wiki/Chinese_in_Los_Angeles "Chinese in Los Angeles") and [in New York City](/wiki/Chinese_in_New_York_City "Chinese in New York City"). Minnan is the most widely-spoken branch of Min, with approximately 34 million native speakers as of 2025.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

The most widely spoken Southern Min language is [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), which includes [Taiwanese](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien").
Other varieties of Southern Min have significant differences from Hokkien, some having limited [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with it, others almost none. [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect"), [Longyan](/wiki/Longyan_Min "Longyan Min"), and [Zhenan](/wiki/Zhenan_Min "Zhenan Min") are said to have general mutual intelligibility with Hokkien, sharing similar phonology and vocabulary to a large extent.[[7]](#cite_note-8) On the other hand, variants such as [Datian](/wiki/Datian_Min "Datian Min"), [Zhongshan](/wiki/Zhongshan_Min "Zhongshan Min"), and [Qiong](/wiki/Hainanese_dialect "Hainanese dialect")-[Lei](/wiki/Leizhou_Min "Leizhou Min") have historical linguistic roots with Hokkien, but are significantly divergent from it in terms of phonology and vocabulary, and thus have almost no mutual intelligibility with Hokkien. Linguists tend to classify them as separate languages.

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: China")]

Southern Min dialects are spoken in southern [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"), specifically in the [cities](/wiki/List_of_administrative_divisions_of_Fujian "List of administrative divisions of Fujian") of [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), [Quanzhou](/wiki/Quanzhou "Quanzhou"), [Zhangzhou](/wiki/Zhangzhou "Zhangzhou"), and much of [Longyan](/wiki/Longyan "Longyan"), hence the name. In addition, varieties of Southern Min are spoken in several southeastern counties of [Wenzhou](/wiki/Wenzhou "Wenzhou") in [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), the [Zhoushan archipelago](/wiki/Zhoushan_archipelago "Zhoushan archipelago") off [Ningbo](/wiki/Ningbo "Ningbo") in [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), the town of [Sanxiang](/wiki/Sanxiang "Sanxiang") at the southern periphery of [Zhongshan](/wiki/Zhongshan "Zhongshan") in [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"),[[8]](#cite_note-Bodman_1985-9) parts of [Huizhou](/wiki/Huizhou "Huizhou") and [Shanwei](/wiki/Shanwei "Shanwei") in Guangdong and in the [Chaoshan](/wiki/Chaoshan "Chaoshan") (Teo-swa) region in Guangdong.

The variant spoken in [Leizhou](/wiki/Leizhou "Leizhou"), Guangdong as well as that in [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan") is classified as [Hainanese](/wiki/Hainanese "Hainanese") and is not mutually intelligible with mainstream Southern Min or Teochew.[[9]](#cite_note-Chappell2019-10) Hainanese is classified in some schemes as part of Southern Min and in other schemes as separate;[[9]](#cite_note-Chappell2019-10) among the latter, Hou combined Hainanese with Leizhou Min in a Qiong–Lei subgroup within Min, distinct from Southern Min.[[10]](#cite_note-hou238-11) Some have even considered this distinction to be at the same level as the [Coastal Min](/wiki/Min_Chinese#Coastal_Min "Min Chinese") – [Inland Min](/wiki/Min_Chinese#Inland_Min "Min Chinese") distinction.[[9]](#cite_note-Chappell2019-10)

[Puxian Min](/wiki/Puxian_Min "Puxian Min") was originally based on the [Quanzhou dialect](/wiki/Quanzhou_dialect "Quanzhou dialect"), but over time became heavily influenced by [Eastern Min](/wiki/Eastern_Min "Eastern Min"), eventually losing intelligibility with Southern Min. It is thus categorised into its own branch alongside Southern Min and Eastern Min.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

The Southern Min dialects spoken in Taiwan, collectively known as [Taiwanese](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien"), is a [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language") for most of the [Hoklo people](/wiki/Hoklo_people "Hoklo people"), the main ethnic group of Taiwan. The correspondence between language and ethnicity is not absolute, as some [Hoklo Taiwanese](/wiki/Hoklo_Taiwanese "Hoklo Taiwanese") people have very limited proficiency in Taiwanese while some non-Hoklo [Taiwanese people](/wiki/Taiwanese_people "Taiwanese people") (including [Hakkas](/wiki/Hakka_people "Hakka people") and [Indigenous](/wiki/Taiwanese_indigenous_peoples "Taiwanese indigenous peoples")) speak Taiwanese Southern Min fluently.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

### Southeast Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Southeast Asia")]

There are many Southern Min speakers among [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). Many ethnic [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") immigrants to the region were [Hoklo](/wiki/Hoklo_people "Hoklo people") from southern Fujian and brought the language to what is now present-day [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") (formerly [British Malaya](/wiki/British_Malaya "British Malaya"), the [Straits Settlements](/wiki/Straits_Settlements "Straits Settlements"), and [British Borneo](/wiki/British_Borneo "British Borneo")), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") (the former [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies")), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") (former [Spanish East Indies](/wiki/Spanish_East_Indies "Spanish East Indies") and later, [US -Philippine Islands](/wiki/Insular_Government_of_the_Philippine_Islands "Insular Government of the Philippine Islands")), [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") (former part of [British Borneo](/wiki/British_Borneo "British Borneo")), [Southern Thailand](/wiki/Southern_Thailand "Southern Thailand"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") ([British Burma](/wiki/British_rule_in_Burma "British rule in Burma")), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") (former [French Cambodia](/wiki/French_protectorate_of_Cambodia "French protectorate of Cambodia") of [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina")), [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Vietnam "Southern Vietnam") [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") (former [French Cochinchina](/wiki/French_Cochinchina "French Cochinchina") of [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina")) and [Central](/wiki/Central_Vietnam "Central Vietnam") [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") (former [French Annam](/wiki/Annam_(French_protectorate) "Annam (French protectorate)") of [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina")). In general, Southern Min from southern Fujian is known as [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), Hokkienese, Fukien, or Fookien in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and is mostly mutually intelligible with Hokkien spoken elsewhere. Many [Southeast Asian](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") ethnic Chinese also originated in the [Chaoshan](/wiki/Chaoshan "Chaoshan") region of [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") and speak [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_language "Teochew language"), the variant of Southern Min from that region, particularly [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Southern Vietnam](/wiki/Southern_Vietnam "Southern Vietnam"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), etc.

In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Philippine Hokkien](/wiki/Philippine_Hokkien "Philippine Hokkien") is reportedly the [native](/wiki/First_language "First language") or [heritage language](/wiki/Heritage_language "Heritage language") of up to 98.7% of the [Chinese Filipino](/wiki/Chinese_Filipino "Chinese Filipino") community, who refer to it as "Lannang" (*Lán-nâng-ōe*; lit. 'our people's language').

Southern Min speakers form the majority of Chinese in Singapore, with Hokkien being the largest group and the second largest being [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_people "Teochew people"). Despite the similarities, the two groups are rarely viewed together as "Southern Min".

## Classification and varieties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Classification and varieties")]

There are two or three major divisions of Southern Min, depending on the criteria for Leizhou and Hainanese inclusion:

* **Southern Min**
  + [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") under the Quanzhang division (泉漳片)
  + [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect"), [Swatow](/wiki/Swatow_dialect "Swatow dialect"), [Jieyang](/wiki/Jieyang "Jieyang"), [Haklau Min](/wiki/Haklau_Min "Haklau Min") under the Chaoshan division (潮汕片)
  + [Leizhou](/wiki/Leizhou_Min "Leizhou Min") and [Hainanese](/wiki/Hainanese "Hainanese") dialects under the Qiong-Lei division (瓊雷片).

More recently, Kwok (2018: 157)[[13]](#cite_note-14) has proposed an alternative classification, with a divergent Northern branch that includes [Quanzhou dialect](/wiki/Quanzhou_dialect "Quanzhou dialect") but not [Zhangzhou dialect](/wiki/Zhangzhou_dialect "Zhangzhou dialect"), as shown below:

* **Southern Min**
  + **Northern**
    - [Cangnan](/wiki/Cangnan "Cangnan")
    - [Quanzhou](/wiki/Quanzhou "Quanzhou"), [Zihu](/wiki/Yushan_County "Yushan County"), [Lukang](/wiki/Lukang "Lukang")
  + **Central-Southern**
    - **Central**
      * [Zhangzhou](/wiki/Zhangzhou "Zhangzhou")
      * [Longyan](/wiki/Longyan "Longyan"), [Datian](/wiki/Datian_County "Datian County")
    - **Southern**
      * Guangdong
        + ? [Haifeng](/wiki/Haifeng "Haifeng")
        + [Jieyang](/wiki/Jieyang "Jieyang"), [Chaoyang](/wiki/Chaoyang_District,_Shantou "Chaoyang District, Shantou")
      * Hainan
        + [Leizhou](/wiki/Leizhou "Leizhou")
        + [Haikou](/wiki/Haikou "Haikou")

### Hokkien

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hokkien")]

Main article: [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien")

Hokkien is the most widely spoken form of Southern Min, including [Amoy dialect](/wiki/Amoy_dialect "Amoy dialect") and [Taiwanese](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien"). Both of these developed as a combination of [Quanzhou](/wiki/Quanzhou_dialect "Quanzhou dialect") and [Zhangzhou](/wiki/Zhangzhou_dialect "Zhangzhou dialect") speech.
Varieties in South-East Asia include: [Singaporean Hokkien](/wiki/Singaporean_Hokkien "Singaporean Hokkien"), [Southern Peninsular Malaysian Hokkien](/wiki/Southern_Peninsular_Malaysian_Hokkien "Southern Peninsular Malaysian Hokkien"), and [Philippine Hokkien](/wiki/Philippine_Hokkien "Philippine Hokkien") (which are closer to Quanzhou Hokkien), and [Penang Hokkien](/wiki/Penang_Hokkien "Penang Hokkien") and [Medan Hokkien](/wiki/Medan_Hokkien "Medan Hokkien") (which are closer to Zhangzhou Hokkien).

### Teochew

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Teochew")]

Main article: [Teochew Min](/wiki/Teochew_Min "Teochew Min")

Teochew is a closely related to Hokkien, with several variants spoken across the [Chaoshan](/wiki/Chaoshan "Chaoshan") region. Some also consider [Haklau Min](/wiki/Haklau_Min "Haklau Min") to be part of Teochew. Despite the close relationship, Teochew and Hokkien are different enough in both pronunciation and vocabulary that mutual intelligibility is difficult.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

### Other varieties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other varieties")]

[Zhenan Min](/wiki/Zhenan_Min "Zhenan Min"), a dialect island in [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") province, is closely related to Quanzhou Hokkien.

[Haklau Min](/wiki/Haklau_Min "Haklau Min"), spoken around [Shanwei](/wiki/Shanwei "Shanwei") and [Haifeng](/wiki/Haifeng "Haifeng"), differs markedly from neighbouring [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect") and may represent a later migration from Zhangzhou. Linguistically, it lies between Teochew and Amoy.

[Datian Min](/wiki/Datian_Min "Datian Min"), spoken in [Datian County](/wiki/Datian_County "Datian County") in [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") province, has been influenced by other Min varieties.

[Sanxiang Min](/wiki/Sanxiang_Min "Sanxiang Min") is spoken in a dialect island in [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") province.

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Main articles: [Hokkien § Phonology](/wiki/Hokkien#Phonology "Hokkien"), and [Teochew dialect § Phonetics and phonology](/wiki/Teochew_dialect#Phonetics_and_phonology "Teochew dialect")

Southern Min has one of the most diverse phonologies of Chinese varieties, with more consonants than Mandarin or Cantonese. Vowels, on the other hand, are more-or-less similar to those of Mandarin. In general, Southern Min dialects have five to six [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"), and [tone sandhi](/wiki/Tone_sandhi "Tone sandhi") is extensive. There are minor variations within Hokkien, and the Teochew system differs somewhat more.

Southern Min's [nasal](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant") [finals](/wiki/Final_(linguistics) "Final (linguistics)") consist of /m/, /n/, /ŋ/, and /~/.

### Sino-Xenic comparisons

Southern Min can trace its origins through the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), and it also has roots from earlier periods. [Hokkien people](/wiki/Hokkien_people "Hokkien people") call themselves "[Tang](/wiki/Names_of_China#Tang "Names of China") people", (**Tn̂g-lâng** [唐人](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%94%90%E4%BA%BA "zh:唐人")/[唐儂](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%94%90%E5%84%82 "zh:唐儂")) which is synonymous to "Chinese people". Because of the widespread influence of the Tang culture during the [Great Tang](/wiki/Great_Tang "Great Tang") dynasty, there are today still many Southern Min pronunciations of words shared by the [Sino-xenic pronunciations](/wiki/Sino-Xenic_vocabularies "Sino-Xenic vocabularies") of [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"), [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language") and [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") languages.

Both Hokkien and Teochew have romanized writing systems and also respective [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters"). In [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), it is known as *Bân-lâm-bûn* (閩南文), while in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), written Hokkien is known as *Tâi-bûn* (台文). Chinese characters are known in China and Taiwan as *Hàn-jī* (漢字). In Malaysia and Singapore, they are known as *Tn̂g-lâng-jī* (唐儂字 / 唐人字). In the Philippines, they are known as *Lán-nâng-lī* (咱儂字 / 咱人字) or *Hàn-bûn-lī* (漢文字).

The use of Chinese characters to write Hokkien remained largely unsystematic in the Ming and Qing dynasties, when characters were used to transcribe colloquial Southern Min speech in opera scripts, folk stories, and regional texts. Among the earliest extant vernacular Southern Min texts using Chinese characters is the *[Tale of the Lychee Mirror](/wiki/Tale_of_the_Lychee_Mirror "Tale of the Lychee Mirror")* ([traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 荔鏡記; [simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 荔镜记; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Lì Jìng Jì*; [Pe̍h-ōe-jī](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "Pe̍h-ōe-jī"): *Nāi-kèng-kì / Lē-kèng-kì*), written in a mix of Hokkien and Teochew. Its earliest extant manuscript dates from 1566.[[16]](#cite_note-Chappell_Peyraube-17)[[17]](#cite_note-Lien-18)

Concurrently, Hokkien interaction with Dominican missionaries based in the [Philippines](/wiki/History_of_the_Philippines_(1565%E2%80%931898) "History of the Philippines (1565–1898)") led to the translation of Spanish doctrinal literature into Hokkien in Roman script.[[18]](#cite_note-Hompot-19) Early 19th century Protestant missionaries, mostly from Britain and originally based in [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca"), developed a different set of romanization schemes independently. This started with the works of [Walter Henry Medhurst](/wiki/Walter_Henry_Medhurst "Walter Henry Medhurst"), later refined by [Samuel Wells Williams](/wiki/Samuel_Wells_Williams "Samuel Wells Williams") and [Elihu Doty](/wiki/Elihu_Doty "Elihu Doty"), and culminated with the script *[Pe̍h-ōe-jī](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "Pe̍h-ōe-jī")* (POJ) as promulgated by [John Van Nest Talmage](/wiki/John_Van_Nest_Talmage "John Van Nest Talmage"), traditionally regarded as the founder of POJ.[[18]](#cite_note-Hompot-19) After the [Treaty of Nanking](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking") was signed in 1842, the center of the writing and publishing of church literature in Southern Min shifted to [Amoy](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), cementing its status as the *de facto* standard for Southern Min.[[18]](#cite_note-Hompot-19)[[19]](#cite_note-Kloter-20) When [Thomas Barclay](/wiki/Thomas_Barclay_(missionary) "Thomas Barclay (missionary)") produced the first printed newspaper in Taiwan, the *[Taiwan Prefectural City Church News](/wiki/Taiwan_Church_News "Taiwan Church News")*, it showed the establishment of a strong tradition of literacy in Hokkien POJ.[[19]](#cite_note-Kloter-20) The success of POJ resulted in its adaptation into *[Pe̍h-ūe-jī](/wiki/Teochew_Romanization "Teochew Romanization")* for Teochew in 1875.

Under [Japanese rule](/wiki/Taiwan_under_Japanese_rule "Taiwan under Japanese rule"), POJ was suppressed and then outlawed, with [Taiwanese kana](/wiki/Taiwanese_kana "Taiwanese kana") becoming the dominant script for Taiwanese Hokkien, although its role in daily life was much reduced.[[19]](#cite_note-Kloter-20) Although after World War II, the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") initially had a liberal attitude towards Southern Min, the use of POJ was put under ever increasing restrictions, leading to an outright prohibition in the 1970s.[[19]](#cite_note-Kloter-20)

With the lifting of martial law and Taiwan’s democratization in the late 1980s and 1990s, the use of Taiwanese Hokkien increased, and various new romanizations were devised.[[19]](#cite_note-Kloter-20) In 2006, the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Education (Taiwan)") of Taiwan officially selected one orthography, often known as [Tâi-Lô](/wiki/T%C3%A2i-u%C3%A2n_L%C3%B4-m%C3%A1-j%C4%AB_Phing-im_Hong-%C3%A0n "Tâi-uân Lô-má-jī Phing-im Hong-àn"), for pedagogical use in the school system. The following year, it released the first list of [Taiwanese Southern Min Recommended Characters](/wiki/Taiwanese_Southern_Min_Recommended_Characters "Taiwanese Southern Min Recommended Characters"), with subsequent lists providing further standardization of the Chinese characters used.[[19]](#cite_note-Kloter-20)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Min&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: History")]

The Min homeland of Fujian was opened to Han Chinese settlement by the defeat of the [Minyue](/wiki/Minyue "Minyue") state by the armies of [Emperor Wu of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Han "Emperor Wu of Han") in 110 BC.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1991328-21) The area features rugged mountainous terrain, with short rivers that flow into the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea"). Most subsequent migration from north to south China passed through the valleys of the [Xiang](/wiki/Xiang_River "Xiang River") and [Gan](/wiki/Gan_River_(Jiangxi) "Gan River (Jiangxi)") rivers to the west, so that Min varieties have experienced less northern influence than other southern groups.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988210,_228-22) As a result, whereas most [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") can be treated as derived from [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese"), the language described by [rhyme dictionaries](/wiki/Rime_dictionary "Rime dictionary") such as the *[Qieyun](/wiki/Qieyun "Qieyun")* (601 AD), Min varieties contain traces of older distinctions.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988228–229-23) Linguists estimate that the oldest layers of Min dialects diverged from the rest of Chinese around the time of the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETing19839–10-24)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaxterSagart201433,_79-25) However, significant waves of migration from the [North China Plain](/wiki/North_China_Plain "North China Plain") occurred.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYan2006120-26) These include:

* The [Uprising of the Five Barbarians](/wiki/Uprising_of_the_Five_Barbarians "Uprising of the Five Barbarians") during the [Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(265%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (265–420)"), particularly the [Disaster of Yongjia](/wiki/Disaster_of_Yongjia "Disaster of Yongjia") in 311 AD, caused a tide of immigration to the south.
* In 669, [Chen Zheng](/wiki/Chen_Zheng_(Tang_dynasty) "Chen Zheng (Tang dynasty)") and his son [Chen Yuanguang](/wiki/Chen_Yuanguang "Chen Yuanguang") from [Gushi County](/wiki/Gushi_County "Gushi County") in [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan") set up a regional administration in [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") to suppress an insurrection by the [She people](/wiki/She_people "She people").
* [Wang Chao](/wiki/Wang_Chao_(Tang_dynasty) "Wang Chao (Tang dynasty)"), also from Gushi, moved south to Fujian and was appointed its governor in 893, near the end of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), and brought tens of thousands of troops from [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan"). In 909, following the fall of the Tang dynasty, his younger brother [Wang Shenzhi](/wiki/Wang_Shenzhi "Wang Shenzhi") founded the [Min Kingdom](/wiki/Min_(Ten_Kingdoms) "Min (Ten Kingdoms)"), one of the [Ten Kingdoms](/wiki/Ten_Kingdoms "Ten Kingdoms") in the [Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period](/wiki/Five_Dynasties_and_Ten_Kingdoms_period "Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period").

[Jerry Norman](/wiki/Jerry_Norman_(sinologist) "Jerry Norman (sinologist)") identifies four main layers in the vocabulary of modern Min varieties:

1. A non-Chinese substratum from the [original languages of Minyue](/wiki/Minyue_language "Minyue language"), which Norman and [Mei Tsu-lin](/wiki/Mei_Tsu-lin "Mei Tsu-lin") believe were [Austroasiatic](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENormanMei1976-27)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1991331–332-28)
2. The earliest Chinese layer, brought to Fujian by settlers from [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") to the north during the Han dynasty.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1991334–336-29)
3. A layer from the [Northern and Southern Dynasties](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_Dynasties "Northern and Southern Dynasties") period, which is largely consistent with the phonology of the *Qieyun* dictionary.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1991336-30)
4. A [literary layer](/wiki/Literary_and_colloquial_readings_of_Chinese_characters "Literary and colloquial readings of Chinese characters") based on the [koiné](/wiki/Koin%C3%A9_language "Koiné language") of [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an"), the capital of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1991337-31)
